chunk_summary: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) affects 11.6% of Indian children under 5, with drivers including inadequate diet, infections, and poor WASH practices. Integrated prevention and treatment are essential to address wasting effectively.
learning_objectives: Understand the population burden of moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in India based on NFHS-5 data.
Identify clinically actionable determinants of MAM, including dietary and health-related factors.
Learn programmatic approaches for integrated prevention and treatment of wasting in community and clinical settings.
key_facts: 19.3% of Indian children under 5 are wasted, and 7.7% are severely wasted (NFHS-5, 2019–2021).
Moderate wasting is estimated at 11.6% by subtracting severely wasted from total wasted.
Key drivers include inadequate diet quality/quantity, recurrent infections, and suboptimal WASH practices.
Wasting is sensitive to short-term shocks like food insecurity and seasonal infection peaks.
Clinically, MAM management requires addressing both dietary and health-related drivers in parallel.
reference_quote: The NFHS-5 (2019–2021) fact sheet reports 19.3% of under-5 children are wasted and 7.7% are severely wasted.
document_key_topics: Population burden of acute wasting in India
Clinically actionable determinants of moderate acute malnutrition
Integrated prevention and treatment strategies for wasting
